learning_outcomes.description: Reflect and report on the philosophical and historical origins of central debates in philosophy.
Demonstrate advanced skills of effective thinking and communication.
Conduct independent philosophical research and inquiry.
Identify, analyse and evaluate complex arguments or points of view.
Develop an appreciation of diverse reasoned perspectives and engage in collaborative reasoning.

assessments.due_date: Work produced in tutorials is due in or at the end of tutorials as instructed by the tutor.
assessments.mode: Activity/ Performance, Oral, Product/ Artefact/ Multimedia, Written
assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Tutorial/ Problem Set
assessments.task_description:
Tutorial work involves two components: (A) Tutorial participation (10%) and (B) 4x in-class activities (4 x 5%=20%). (Students with an accommodation must contact the course coordinator to arrange alternatives.) The first tutorial in week 1 reviews argument mapping, nutshell assignments, and asks students to complete a survey as part of a student-staff partnership. Graded tutorials run for the following 10 weeks (2-6 and 8-12).

(A) Tutorial work enables steady development of skills in philosophical analysis and critical thinking. A Community of Inquiry approach is taken where students work together to solve problems. Students will be judged on their ability to work collaboratively to progress arguments, generate critical questions and prompts, and discuss competing interpretations and points of view. 

Tutorials are mandatory and attendance will be taken. Marks are not awarded for attendance but for contributions to collective discussion and completion of set tasks. Alternative or make-up work will be assigned to students who miss a tutorial due to documented medical or other legitimate reasons. 

During tutorials, students are expected to:

1. Demonstrate evidence of comprehension of the texts assigned for reading each week through preparation and participation in the tutorial. Required readings will be marked 'required' and made available through the Library.

2. Come to class with work prepared as assigned the week before each tutorial. This course integrates an optional use of Generative AI as part of the preparation for tutorials.

3. Conduct in-class analyses of short passages.

(B) 4 x in-class activities are comprised of:

  1. Mini nutshell #1 (week 4)
  2. Socratic circle (week 5)
  3. Socratic circle (week 10)
  4. Pitch event (weeks 11 & 12)


Copies (e.g., photographs) of in-class work during these exercises must be uploaded by the student to the relevant Turnitin site at the end of the tutorial to obtain a grade.

See Tutorial Participation and Exercises for more information about these tasks.

Note on the use of AI:

This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You must only use the approved materials and tools for this assessment task. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct. See assessment instructions for more details.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Copies (e.g., photographs) of student artefacts produced during in-class work during these exercises must be uploaded by the student to the relevant Turnitin site at the end of the tutorial to obtain a grade.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
In the Nutshell  assignment (20%; 500 words) students are asked to contextualise, analyse, explain, and evaluate an argument contained in the text provided. Students will identify the structure of the argument (the relationship between premises and conclusion) in an argument map including the base argument and supporting reasons or hidden assumptions. Students will append a short statement contextualising the argument (i.e., situating it against an opposing view) and a short evaluation (identifying one major weakness or flaw in the argument). These are essential skills in the history of philosophy.

This test will be conducted and submitted in 30 minutes during the lecture in week 6. Students will be informed of the text from which the argument is drawn in the week prior to the test.

Criteria & Marking: 

Your nutshell assignments will be assessed according to the following criteria: 

1. Ability to define the topic or philosophical issue under debate. (Topic definition.)

2. Ability to identify and analyse the structure of an argument for a certain conclusion. (Argument and Structure.)

3. Depth and breadth of understanding of the point at issue. (Scope and Depth of Treatment)

4. Insight and/or originality in analysing or argument. (Originality)

5. Capacity to produce a polished, well-written analysis and/or evaluation. (Presentation)


 Note on the use of AI:

This assessment is designed to protect academic integrity and assess your understanding of course concepts. You must only use the approved materials and tools for this assessment task. Use of prohibited materials in this task may be investigated and treated as academic misconduct. See assessment instructions for more details.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Select ONE topic from the Trialogue Task sheet available on Blackboard or design your own primary research question (with approval from the lecturers). Preparatory work for this assignment must be conducted in tutorials.

This is a TWO-PART Assignment:

Part A: Research design component (600 words total; 15%):

  1. Define the philosophical problem and key terms. (50 words)
  2. Generate secondary research questions. Either select a primary research question from the Trialogue Task Sheet or design your own (with approval from the lecturers). Label this 'RQ1'. To narrow your research focus, generate 2-3 secondary research questions related to specific aspects of the problem you are interested in investigating. Label these 'SRQ1', 'SRQ2', etc. Explain why these are significant and relevant secondary research questions to explore. (100 words)
  3. Locate 4 credible secondary sources reflecting different sides of the debate in the early modern period. Use the Library catalogue or Google Scholar to find sources. Read the articles to ensure relevance and significance. Cite the articles using APA7 and explain why each is relevant, significant, and credible. (See the CRAAP test for judging the credibility of sources). [NB: Secondary sources are articles or books written by scholars of early modern philosophy about the philosophical texts studied in this course.] (4 x 50 words per entry (excluding the citation) = 200 words in total.)
  4. Design a strong thesis statement. (50 words)
  5. Create an argument map of your trialogue. (200 words)


Part B: Pitch event (3 minutes; 5%)

Students will pitch their arguments in a 3 minute presentation either in week 11 or 12. Students will be randomly assigned to a week and so all must be ready to present by week 10. (See Research Design Task sheet for more information about the pitch event.)

Note on the use of AI:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

In this assessment you can use resources such as your notes, course materials, and other tools, including AI, while completing the task. The focus is not on memorising information, but on understanding, applying judgement, and demonstrating your own thinking.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessments.task_description:
Imagine you are living in the early modern period and have the opportunity to talk philosophy with two of the philosophers we have studied in this course. The two thinkers you choose to write about must have divergent views on one of the topics from the course. Your job is to write a trialogue based on this philosophical encounter between yourself and these two figures. You must capture the dialectic between the opponents, adjudicate between their strongest arguments, and formulate your own answer to the question they are debating based on your own reasoning and evaluation of their arguments.

This is an exercise in philosophical storytelling. It should have a good narrative structure and engage the reader both philosophically and from a literary perspective.

Maximum word limit: 1500 words (excluding bibliography).

Weight: 30% (Note: Any assignment over 15% must be attempted to obtain a final grade in the course.)

Due date: November 9, 2026. 23:59.

Submission information: Submit to ‘Trialogue’ Turnitin Assignment under ‘Assessment’ on Blackboard. Obtain a receipt as proof of submission.

The School of Historical and Philosophical Inquiry will not accept screenshots as proof work was completed by the due date or as evidence of failed uploads through Turnitin. It is your responsibility to submit to Turnitin by the due date. Any late submission will be subject to late submission penalties. (See section 5.3 of the electronic course profile for further information.)

Extensions due to medical emergencies need to be applied for through the School prior to the due date. For information about extensions, see https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/exams-and-assessment/applying-extension

REQUIREMENTS:

1.   Students should consult primary sources (texts written by the original historical figures looked at in the course—e.g., Descartes, Spinoza, etc.,) and secondary sources (scholarly analyses of these texts). Good trialogues will often consult 8-10 credible, relevant, and significant sources. Do not list sources in your bibliography that you do not use or cite in the body of your dialogue.

2.    The trialogue must come to a conclusion about the topic—it should offer a reasonable justification in support of a thesis.

3.  The trialogue should be well-written, checked for spelling and grammar, and must exhibit a high standard of referencing that is used consistently (e.g. APA7). Include in-text references and page numbers for any attributions of ideas and/or quotes.

4. Students must comply with academic misconduct and integrity policies. It is mandatory for all students to complete two academic and misconduct integrity modules to receive a grade in courses (including PHIL2013) in the School of Historical and Philosophical Inquiry. If you have not completed these modules, please do so through Academic Integrity Modules (AIM).

5. See the Philosophy Criteria Rubric attached under 'Course Files' for more information about the grading criteria and standards.

For a guide to writing a philosophical dialogue or trialogue, see https://owlcation.com/humanities/How-to-write-a-Philosophy-Dialogue.

Note on the use of AI:

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. While students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance.

In this assessment you can use resources such as your notes, course materials, and other tools, including AI, while completing the task. The focus is not on memorising information, but on understanding, applying judgement, and demonstrating your own thinking.

Academic Misconduct: Referencing

Fabrication or misrepresentation of sources is considered a breach of academic integrity and may be referred for academic misconduct, regardless of performance in other criteria. This includes but is not limited to: citing non-existent sources, attributing false quotes to real sources, or not including accurate page numbers.

learning_activities.topic:
Philosophy in transition

This seminar focuses on the crucial shifts in thinking that mark the distinction between the philosophies of antiquity and the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period, in particular, the departure from the form/matter and nature/art distinctions of Aristotelianism.

An additional tutorial will be offered in this week to students to review argumentation skills, run through the nutshell assignment, and complete the pre-survey for the student-staff partnership.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Philosophy in transition This seminar focuses on the crucial shifts in thinking that mark the distinction between the philosophies of antiquity and the Middle Ages and the Early Modern period, in particular, the departure from the form/matter and nature/art distinctions of Aristotelianism. An additional tutorial will be offered in this week to students to review argumentation skills, run through the nutshell assignment, and complete the pre-survey for the student-staff partnership.

learning_activities.topic:
Metaphysical Themes

The philosophers of the 17th and 18th century developed distinctive metaphysical ideas about the building blocks of reality--substances; modes; souls; ideas--and ideas about how things causally interact if they do at all. This week we look at the major metaphysical theories on offer and the reasons behind them.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Metaphysical Themes The philosophers of the 17th and 18th century developed distinctive metaphysical ideas about the building blocks of reality--substances; modes; souls; ideas--and ideas about how things causally interact if they do at all. This week we look at the major metaphysical theories on offer and the reasons behind them.

learning_activities.topic:
No class: Ekka holiday

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Causation and Agency

An examination of different early modern approaches to natural and divine causation and the implications for human agency.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Causation and Agency An examination of different early modern approaches to natural and divine causation and the implications for human agency.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 5 To Week 5(24 Aug - 30 Aug)
learning_activities.topic:
Life

An examination of the distinction between living and non-living things under the shift to a mechanical philosophy, as well as debates about functions, systems, and animal consciousness and thought.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Life An examination of the distinction between living and non-living things under the shift to a mechanical philosophy, as well as debates about functions, systems, and animal consciousness and thought.

learning_activities.topic:
Thinking Matter

An examination of post-Cartesian debates about the possibility of thinking matter and implications for the nature of the human being.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Thinking Matter An examination of post-Cartesian debates about the possibility of thinking matter and implications for the nature of the human being.

learning_activities.topic:
Human Nature

This week examines debates about the nature of the human being--what kind of union exists between the mind and body and what that means for our relationship to each other and the natural world.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Human Nature This week examines debates about the nature of the human being--what kind of union exists between the mind and body and what that means for our relationship to each other and the natural world.

learning_activities.topic:
Passions and Virtues

This week begins the exploration of the relationship between those lynchpins of mind-body union--the passions--and the characteristics of humans that enable it to pursue the good.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Passions and Virtues This week begins the exploration of the relationship between those lynchpins of mind-body union--the passions--and the characteristics of humans that enable it to pursue the good.

learning_activities.topic:
Virtue and the passions: Spinoza

This seminar consider about the nature and function of the passions and their role in constituting the union of mind and body in the philosophy of Spinoza.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Virtue and the passions: Spinoza This seminar consider about the nature and function of the passions and their role in constituting the union of mind and body in the philosophy of Spinoza.

learning_activities.topic:
Egoism and Morality

This seminar focuses on how thinkers in the early modern period developed ethical theories from their natural philosophical perspectives on the nature and function of the passions and the tension between egoistic and moral motivations to action.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Egoism and Morality This seminar focuses on how thinkers in the early modern period developed ethical theories from their natural philosophical perspectives on the nature and function of the passions and the tension between egoistic and moral motivations to action.

learning_activities.topic:
The equality of the sexes

This seminar explores early modern debates about equality between the sexes and their relationship to broader philosophical worldviews in the period.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: The equality of the sexes This seminar explores early modern debates about equality between the sexes and their relationship to broader philosophical worldviews in the period.

learning_activities.topic:
Political psychology

This week compares the political psychologies of Hobbes and Spinoza. We consider the central role played by the passions of hope and fear in Hobbes's and Spinoza's accounts of the constitution of political society, and also consider the rationale for their different proposals regarding the use of these passions as a political tool for governing subjects. 

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Political psychology This week compares the political psychologies of Hobbes and Spinoza. We consider the central role played by the passions of hope and fear in Hobbes's and Spinoza's accounts of the constitution of political society, and also consider the rationale for their different proposals regarding the use of these passions as a political tool for governing subjects.

learning_activities.topic:
Reception

This seminar considers the influence of early modern debates on current debates in moral and political philosophy.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Reception This seminar considers the influence of early modern debates on current debates in moral and political philosophy.
